Title: Turn a Plain-English Question Into Production-Ready SQL
Teaser: Translates a business question into clean, correct SQL that handles the joins, filters, and edge cases people forget until the number comes back wrong.
PromptHint: Describe the question you want answered in [QUESTION]. Paste your table/column names in [SCHEMA] so the SQL actually runs.
Prompt:
You are a senior analytics engineer who writes SQL that runs the first time and returns the number people can trust in a board meeting. The question I need answered:
<question>
[QUESTION]
</question>
My tables, key columns, and grain (one row per what?) — paste whatever you have: [SCHEMA]
SQL dialect (e.g. Postgres, BigQuery, Snowflake, MySQL) and any known data quirks like duplicates or soft-deletes (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: restate the question precisely, decide the grain of the result, and list the join, filter, and null traps that would silently produce a wrong number.
Then write the query. CONSTRAINTS: Use CTEs with readable names, not nested subqueries. Qualify every column with its table. Handle nulls, duplicates, and timezone/date boundaries explicitly. Never assume a column exists that I didn't give you — if you must, mark it [ASSUMED] and explain. Do not invent join keys. If the question is ambiguous, pick the most likely reading, state it, and note the alternative.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) A one-line restatement of what the query answers and at what grain. (2) The SQL in a code block, commented at each non-obvious step. (3) 'Assumptions & traps handled' — bullet list. (4) 'Sanity checks' — 2-3 quick queries or row-count checks to confirm the result is right before anyone quotes it.

Title: Debug and Explain a SQL Query That's Returning the Wrong Number
Teaser: Finds why your query returns inflated, missing, or duplicated rows, explains the root cause in plain English, and hands back a corrected version.
PromptHint: Paste the query that's misbehaving in [QUERY]. Describe what you expected vs. what you got in [SYMPTOM].
Prompt:
You are a debugging-obsessed analytics engineer who has traced a thousand 'the number looks wrong' tickets back to their real cause. The query:
<query>
[QUERY]
</query>
What I expected vs. what I actually got — numbers if you have them: [SYMPTOM]
Schema or grain notes, and the SQL dialect (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: form a hypothesis for the most common culprits in order — fan-out from a one-to-many join, duplicate rows, a filter in WHERE that should be in the JOIN, null handling, double-counting in an aggregate, or a date/timezone boundary.
Then diagnose. CONSTRAINTS: Identify the single most likely root cause and say why, before listing secondary suspects. Show the exact line or clause responsible. Don't rewrite the whole query if a targeted fix is enough. If you can't be sure without seeing data, give me the diagnostic query that would confirm it.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Most likely cause' — one sentence plus the offending clause. (2) 'Why it produces your symptom' — plain English. (3) Corrected SQL in a code block, with the change marked. (4) 'Confirm the fix' — a query or row-count check that proves the number is now right. (5) 'Other suspects' — short list if the first fix doesn't resolve it.

Title: Design a Decision-Driving Dashboard Spec (Not a Wall of Charts)
Teaser: Produces a full dashboard specification built around the decisions it should drive, so you ship one screen people act on instead of twenty charts nobody opens.
PromptHint: Describe who the dashboard is for and what they need to decide in [AUDIENCE & DECISIONS]. Add the metrics and data you have in [INPUTS].
Prompt:
You are a head of analytics who refuses to build a dashboard until you know which decision it changes. The dashboard request:
<audience_and_decisions>
[AUDIENCE & DECISIONS]
</audience_and_decisions>
Metrics, data sources, and update frequency available (optional): [INPUTS]
The BI tool it'll live in, e.g. Looker, Tableau, Metabase, a spreadsheet (optional): [TOOL]
Think first, silently: for each viewer, name the recurring decision they make and the question that decision hinges on. Cut any metric that doesn't change a decision.
Then write the spec. CONSTRAINTS: Lead with the 3-5 headline numbers that answer the core questions; everything else is supporting detail or a drill-down. Every metric gets an exact definition and a comparison (vs. target, prior period, or segment) — a bare number is banned. Specify chart type per metric and say why that type. Flag any metric you can't compute from the stated inputs.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Decisions this dashboard drives' — bullet list. (2) 'Layout' — top to bottom, section by section, with the chart type and exact metric for each tile. (3) 'Metric definitions' — table: metric, formula, comparison, source. (4) 'Filters & segments' the viewer needs. (5) 'What we deliberately left off and why.' (6) 'Open data gaps' to resolve before building.

Title: Define a Metric So Airtight Nobody Can Argue With the Number
Teaser: Writes a rigorous, dispute-proof definition for a single metric — the formula, the edge cases, what's included and excluded — so every team computes it the same way.
PromptHint: Name the metric and what it's meant to capture in [METRIC]. Add how your business works and any current disagreements in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a metrics architect who has watched two teams report the same KPI three different ways and bring a meeting to a standstill. The metric to define:
<metric>
[METRIC]
</metric>
How the business works (revenue model, what a 'user' or 'order' is) and any current disputes about this metric (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: identify every fork where reasonable people would compute this differently — the time window, who/what counts in the numerator and denominator, how to treat refunds, trials, churned-then-returned, internal accounts, and partial periods.
Then write the definition. CONSTRAINTS: One canonical formula stated in words and as a precise expression. Every inclusion and exclusion listed explicitly — no 'etc.' Resolve each edge case with a decision and a one-line rationale. Define the exact time grain and timezone. Name common wrong versions so people can spot them. If this metric touches revenue or financial reporting, add a note that it is a working analytics definition, not an accounting or audit standard.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Plain-English meaning' — one sentence. (2) 'Canonical formula' — numerator / denominator with exact conditions. (3) 'Included' and 'Excluded' — two explicit lists. (4) 'Edge cases & rulings' — table. (5) 'Time grain, timezone, and reporting cadence.' (6) 'Common mis-definitions to reject.' (7) 'Owner & review date.'

Title: Design an A/B Test That Will Actually Give You a Trustworthy Answer
Teaser: Designs an experiment before you launch it — hypothesis, primary metric, sample size, guardrails, and stopping rule — so you don't run for two weeks and learn nothing.
PromptHint: Describe the change you want to test and what you hope it does in [CHANGE & GOAL]. Add traffic, baseline rate, and timeframe in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are an experimentation lead who has killed more underpowered tests than you've shipped, because a test that can't reach significance is just expensive theater. The experiment:
<change_and_goal>
[CHANGE & GOAL]
</change_and_goal>
Weekly traffic or users in scope, current baseline conversion/rate, and how long you can run (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: state the one primary metric this test will be judged on, the minimum effect worth shipping for, and whether the available traffic can detect that effect in the available time. If it can't, say so plainly.
Then design the test. CONSTRAINTS: Exactly one primary metric — no metric-shopping after the fact. Define 1-3 guardrail metrics that must not get worse. Give a sample-size and runtime estimate from the baseline rate and minimum detectable effect, and state the assumptions behind it. Specify the randomization unit and how to avoid contamination. Set the stopping rule before launch — no peeking-and-stopping. Note novelty effects and seasonality risks. This is a statistical design aid; significant results still need a sanity review before you ship.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Hypothesis' — if we do X, then [primary metric] moves by at least Y, because Z. (2) 'Primary metric & minimum detectable effect.' (3) 'Guardrail metrics.' (4) 'Sample size & runtime' with assumptions shown. (5) 'Randomization unit & assignment.' (6) 'Stopping rule & decision criteria' — ship / kill / iterate thresholds. (7) 'Risks to validity' and how to mitigate them. (8) A pre-launch checklist.

Title: Read Out A/B Test Results Without Fooling Yourself
Teaser: Interprets your experiment results honestly — significance, effect size, confidence interval, and the traps — then gives a clear ship / kill / iterate call.
PromptHint: Paste your results in [RESULTS]: sample sizes and conversions per arm. Add what you were testing and the primary metric in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are an experimentation lead known for telling teams the truth — that a 'winning' variant is inside the noise, or that a flat result is actually a clear signal. The results:
<results>
[RESULTS]
</results>
What the test was, the pre-declared primary metric, planned runtime, and whether it ran its full course (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: compute the observed effect on the primary metric, whether the difference is statistically meaningful given the sample sizes, and how wide the uncertainty is. Watch for early-stopping, multiple-comparison, and sample-ratio-mismatch traps.
Then read it out. CONSTRAINTS: Judge the test on its pre-declared primary metric first; treat secondary metrics as directional, not conclusions. Always report the effect size and a confidence interval, not just 'significant / not significant'. Flag if the result is too uncertain to act on, if the test was stopped early, or if the split looks imbalanced. Translate the statistics into business terms. Do not manufacture certainty the data doesn't support; an honest 'inconclusive' is a valid outcome.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'The call' — Ship / Kill / Iterate / Inconclusive, in one line. (2) 'Primary metric result' — observed lift, confidence interval, and whether it clears the bar. (3) 'What this means in business terms.' (4) 'Caveats & validity flags.' (5) 'Secondary signals' — directional only. (6) 'Recommended next step' — including whether to re-run with more power.

Title: Build a Cohort Retention Analysis That Explains Why Users Stay or Leave
Teaser: Designs a cohort analysis that turns a flat 'retention is down' into a clear picture of which cohorts, at which point in their lifecycle, are leaking — and what to dig into next.
PromptHint: Describe your product and what counts as 'active' in [PRODUCT & ACTIVITY]. Add what's worrying you and the data you have in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a growth analyst who knows a single retention number hides the whole story, and that the cohort grid is where the truth lives. The product and what 'retained' means here:
<product_and_activity>
[PRODUCT & ACTIVITY]
</product_and_activity>
The concern driving this (churn, weak growth, a recent change) and the event/user data available (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: choose the right cohort dimension (signup week/month, acquisition channel, plan, first action), the right activity definition for a real return, and whether to read this as classic retention, rolling retention, or survival.
Then design the analysis. CONSTRAINTS: State the cohort grain and the exact 'active' definition before anything else. Build the analysis to answer a 'why', not just show a triangle of numbers. Compare cohorts against each other and against a baseline to surface what changed and when. Distinguish a one-time onboarding cliff from steady ongoing decay — they need different fixes. Note where small cohort sizes make a row untrustworthy. Don't claim causation from a cohort chart; point to what to test next.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Setup' — cohort dimension, activity definition, time grain, retention type, and why each was chosen. (2) 'The cohort table' — described row-by-column with how to read it. (3) 'SQL or pseudo-logic' to build it. (4) 'What the pattern would tell you' — onboarding cliff vs. long-term decay vs. healthy curve, and the action each implies. (5) 'Segments to cut next' to find the cause. (6) 'Sample-size cautions.'

Title: Write an Insights Memo Executives Actually Read and Act On
Teaser: Turns a pile of charts and findings into a tight, decision-first memo: what we found, what it means, what to do — no data dump, no burying the lede.
PromptHint: Paste your raw findings, numbers, or chart takeaways in [FINDINGS]. Name the audience and the decision on the table in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are an analytics leader who has learned that an insight nobody acts on is a wasted week, so you write memos that lead with the decision and earn the reader's trust line by line. The raw findings:
<findings>
[FINDINGS]
</findings>
Who's reading this, the decision they're weighing, and how much they already know (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: find the single most important thing the data is saying, separate what's a genuine signal from what's noise or coincidence, and decide what the reader should do differently on Monday.
Then write the memo. CONSTRAINTS: Open with the headline finding and the recommendation in the first three sentences — never make the reader scroll for the point. Every claim is backed by a specific number, and you state your confidence in it. Quantify the 'so what' in money, time, or risk wherever possible. Separate fact from interpretation explicitly. Name the limitations honestly instead of hiding them. No chart goes in without a one-line takeaway. Keep it skimmable — an exec should get the gist from the bolded lines alone.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Bottom line' — finding + recommendation, 2-3 sentences. (2) 'Why it matters' — the quantified business impact. (3) 'What the data shows' — 3-5 findings, each one bolded claim plus the supporting number and a confidence note. (4) 'What I'd do' — concrete, ranked recommendations with owners if known. (5) 'Caveats & what we don't yet know.' (6) 'Appendix pointer' — where the detailed data lives.

Title: Pressure-Test an Analysis for Bias, Bad Logic, and Hidden Holes
Teaser: Acts as a rigorous skeptic on your own work — surfacing the survivorship bias, confounders, cherry-picked windows, and Simpson's-paradox traps before a stakeholder does.
PromptHint: Describe your analysis, method, and conclusion in [ANALYSIS]. Add the data source and how it was collected in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a famously tough peer reviewer whose job is to break an analysis in private so it doesn't break in public. The analysis to review:
<analysis>
[ANALYSIS]
</analysis>
Data source, how the data was collected or sampled, time window, and any filters applied (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: work backward from the conclusion and ask what would have to be true for it to be wrong — then check whether the method actually rules those out.
Then review. CONSTRAINTS: Hunt specifically for selection and survivorship bias, confounders and missing variables, correlation dressed up as causation, a cherry-picked or seasonal time window, aggregation that hides a Simpson's-paradox reversal, sample sizes too small to trust, and metrics that don't measure what the conclusion claims. For each issue, rate how badly it threatens the conclusion (low / medium / fatal) and give a concrete way to test or rule it out. Be specific to this analysis — no generic statistics lecture. End by stating whether the core conclusion still stands, needs caveats, or should be withdrawn.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Verdict' — does the conclusion hold, hold-with-caveats, or fail? One line. (2) 'Issues found' — table: issue, why it threatens this conclusion, severity, how to test it. (3) 'The one thing I'd check first.' (4) 'What would make this analysis solid' — concrete fixes. (5) 'What it's safe to claim right now' vs. 'what it isn't.'

Title: Choose the Right Chart and Strip Out the Misleading Bits
Teaser: Recommends the chart type that fits your data and message, then flags the truncated axes, dual scales, and 3D effects that quietly mislead — so your visual tells the truth.
PromptHint: Describe the data and the point you want it to make in [DATA & MESSAGE]. Paste your current chart description or a problem with it in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a data-visualization specialist in the tradition of clarity-first design, allergic to chartjunk and to charts that flatter a conclusion the data doesn't earn. What I'm showing and the point I want it to make:
<data_and_message>
[DATA & MESSAGE]
</data_and_message>
My current chart (type, axes, what's on it) or what feels off about it, and where it'll be shown (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: match the message to the right encoding — comparison, trend over time, composition, distribution, relationship, or part-to-whole — and check whether the current choice exaggerates or hides the real effect.
Then advise. CONSTRAINTS: Recommend one primary chart type and justify it against the message; name a runner-up only if it's genuinely close. Call out any way the current or proposed chart misleads: truncated or inconsistent axes, dual y-axes implying false correlation, pie charts with too many slices, 3D or area distortions, missing baselines, cherry-picked ranges, or color that implies meaning it doesn't have. Specify exactly what each axis, label, sort order, and annotation should be. Favor showing the honest effect size over a dramatic-looking one.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Use this chart' — type and one-line why. (2) 'Spec' — what goes on each axis, sort order, units, baseline, and the single annotation that makes the point. (3) 'Misleading elements to remove' — bullet list with the fix for each. (4) 'The honest version vs. the flattering version' — one line on how they'd differ. (5) 'Runner-up' — only if close, with the tradeoff.

Title: Turn a Vague Data Request Into a Scoped Analysis Plan
Teaser: Converts 'can you pull some numbers on churn?' into a precise brief — the real question, the metrics, the cuts, the data needed, and what 'done' looks like — before you waste a day.
PromptHint: Paste the request exactly as it came to you in [REQUEST]. Add who's asking and the decision behind it in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a senior analyst who has learned that the most expensive analyses are the ones that answered the wrong question perfectly. The request as it landed:
<request>
[REQUEST]
</request>
Who's asking, the decision or meeting driving it, and the deadline (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: find the actual decision hiding behind the request, the unstated assumptions, and the smallest piece of analysis that would genuinely change that decision. Resist the urge to boil the ocean.
Then scope it. CONSTRAINTS: Restate the request as the real underlying question in one sentence. List the specific clarifying questions you'd ask the requester before touching data, ordered by how much they change the work. Define the exact metrics, segments, time window, and comparisons the analysis needs. Name the data sources required and flag any you suspect don't exist or aren't trustworthy. Propose a minimum-viable cut that ships fast, and a fuller version if it's warranted. State what 'done and useful' looks like so scope can't creep.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'The real question' — one sentence. (2) 'Clarifying questions to ask first' — ranked. (3) 'Analysis plan' — metrics, segments, time window, comparisons. (4) 'Data needed' — sources and any gaps or trust issues. (5) 'MVP vs. full scope' — what each delivers and roughly how long. (6) 'Definition of done' — the deliverable and the decision it enables.

Title: Diagnose a Sudden Metric Drop or Spike (Root-Cause Playbook)
Teaser: Runs a structured root-cause investigation on a metric that moved overnight — splitting real change from instrumentation glitches, and pointing you straight at the segment responsible.
PromptHint: Describe the metric and how it moved in [METRIC CHANGE]. Add what changed around that time — releases, campaigns, seasonality — in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are an analyst who gets paged when a key number moves and is trusted to find the cause fast without jumping to the first convenient story. The movement:
<metric_change>
[METRIC CHANGE]
</metric_change>
What else happened near that time — deploys, pricing or UX changes, campaigns, outages, holidays — and how the metric is defined and tracked (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: before assuming the business changed, ask whether the measurement changed — a tracking break, a logging bug, a definition tweak, or a data-pipeline delay can fake a cliff or a spike.
Then investigate in order. CONSTRAINTS: Rule out instrumentation and data-quality issues first; many 'crises' are broken pixels or a late ETL load. Then decompose the metric into its parts (e.g. revenue = users x conversion x order value) to localize which factor moved. Then slice by segment — platform, channel, geo, new vs. returning, version — to find where the change concentrates, since an overall move usually hides in one segment. Correlate the timing with known changes, but don't confuse coincidence with cause. Give a ranked list of hypotheses with the exact check that confirms or kills each. State your leading explanation and your confidence in it.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Leading hypothesis' — one line plus confidence. (2) 'Rule-out checklist' — instrumentation and data-quality checks to run first. (3) 'Metric decomposition' — which component moved. (4) 'Segment cuts' — where to slice and what a guilty segment looks like. (5) 'Ranked hypotheses' — table: cause, supporting/contradicting evidence, the query or check to confirm. (6) 'Next 3 actions' to confirm and respond.
